F ern P olrrrcAL P necrrcEs L oMMrssroN
-12ti J Street o Suite 620 o Sacrantento. CA 9-581+-l-ll9
(916) 322--s660 o Fax (916) -321-0886
June 27, 2008
James Salvante
Coastal Valley EMS Agency
472 Avration Boulevard, Suite 200
Santa Rosa, California 95,103
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-08-075
Dear Mr. Salvante
This letter responds to your reqnest fbr informal assistance regarding the conflict-
of'-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")l and is based on the facts
presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a
finder of fact when it renders advice. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)
Additionally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not address
the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of
interest or Government Code Section 1090. Becalrse you have not provided enough facts
regarding the activities in which you will be engaged, we offer only informal assistance.2
QUESTIONS
1. Does the fact that your agency has determined that your position should not be
included as a designated position with the agency's conflict of interest code exempt yoll
from complying with the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions?
2. Do you have a conflict of interest tmder the Act, based on your fbrmer
employment, if you serve on an advisory committee making recommendations regarding a
I The Political Retirrm Act is contained in Governnrent Code'sections 8l(XX) throu-rrh glOl;1. All
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Comnrission are contained in Sections lli I l0 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the Calittx'nia
Code of Regulations. All regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the Calitirrnia Code of
Regulations. unless otherr,l'ise indicated.
- Inlirrmal assistance cftres nol proride the rccluestor lrith the immunitl, pro',idecl by un rrpinion or
tirrmal written udvice. (Section ti-lll-ll re_gulation llt329(c)( l). copy enclosedl.


File No. I-08-075
Pa,ee No. 2
Request for Proposals (RFP) under which your former employer will be one of the likely
bidders for the potential contract?
CONCLUSIONS
l. No. The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply to all public officials,
whether or not they are covered in the agency's contlict-of-interest code.
2. No. You will not have a conflict of interest if you do not make, participate in
making, or Llse your official position to influence a govemmental decision that will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of your economic interests, as
explained below. Furthermore, as your former employment was terminated before you
began public service, you do not have an economic interest in your former employer so
long as there was no expectation by you of retlrming to your former job at the time you
accepted you current position and all income from the former employer was received by or
accmed to yoll before you began you cllrrent employment.
rACTS
You are an employee of the County of Sonoma serving in the capacity of
"Advanced Life Support Coordinator" for the Coastal Valleys Emergency Medical Services
Agency, a regional regulatory agency that oversees prehospital medical services including
911 medical systems for Sonoma, Napa, and Mendocino Counties.
Your routine responsibilities include: policy and treatment protocol development;
continuous quality improvement program management; investigation and follow-up with
clinical issues; development and support of on-going training programs; management of the
agency's continuing education provider program; management of the agency's public
safety AED program; oversight of the certification process for EMT-1 providers and the
accreditation process for paramedic providers; ALS ambulance inspections; attendance at
regional and county meetings including the regional Medical Advisory Committee and the
county-specific Emergency Medical Services Advisory Committees for Napa, Sonoma, and
Mendocino Counties.
Pursuant to Section 87302, your employer has enumerated the positions within the
colrnty that involve the making or participation in the making of decisions that may have a
material effect on any financial interest. Several years ago, your employer reviewed your
position and determined that it shor.rld not be included as a designated position.
Consequently, you do not file a Fair Political Practices Form 700 (Statement of Economic
Interests). You have been advised that "this fact ordinarily would preclude [you] from
having a Political Reform Act conflict of interest." However, yoll are reqllesting our advice
regarding this issue.
Sonoma County is in the process of preparing a Request for Proposal (RFP) for
exclttsive rights to operate Advanced Life Support service within the Santa Rosa area


File No. I-08-075
Page No. 3
pursLrant to California Health and Safety Code Section 1797.224. You are being
considered to take part in the group that will make recommendations regarding the RFP
and work on the project. Your work will be supervised by the EMS Agency
Administrator, the EMS Agency Medical Director, the Sonoma County Public Health
Officer, and the Sonoma County Director of Health Services. The ultimate contract
will be reviewed and approved by the Sonoma County Board of Supervisors. You will
be serving in an advisory capacity to the Department of Health Services as an EMS
agency representative and sharing the agency's viewpoint with the project team. You
will not be serving on the selection committee or making recommendations as to which
bidder will be awarded the contract.
You have a previons employment relationship with American Medical Response,
one of the likely bidders for the potential contract. You were employed by AMR through
December 2007. You do not have any other financial relationship with AMR.
ANALYSIS
PorpNrmr, CoNrlrcr oF INTEREST
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or
the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section
87100 prohibits onlt pubhc official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official
has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or
more of his or her financial interests.
SrBp L: Ann You A Punuc OrrrcrlI,?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local governmental agency." Because yoll are an employee of the
County of Sonoma, you are a public official under the Act, and the Act's conflict-of-
interest provisions apply to you. (Section 82048.)
SrBp 2: Wrr,r, You BB MarrNc, PlRrrcrparrNc rN MlrcrNc, oR INFLUENCTNG A
GovnRNpTENTAL DpcrsroN
As a public official you may not make, participate in making, or otherwise Llse your
official position to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on any of your economic interests.


File No. t-08-075
Page No.4
Making a Govemmental Decision:
"A public official 'makes a govemmental decisior,' ...
when the official, acting within the ar.rthority of his or her
office or position:
"( 1) Votes on a matter;
"(2) Appoillts a person;
"(3) Obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of
action;
"(4) Enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his
or her agency;
"(5) Determines not to act, within the meaning of
subdivisions (a)(1), (a)(2), (a)(3), or (a)(4), above, unless
such determination is made because of his or her financial
interest." (Regulation 18702.1.)
Participating in a Governmental Decision:
"A public official 'participates in making a governmental
decision,' ..., when, acting within the authority of his or
her position, the official:
"(a) Negotiates, without significant substantive review,
with a governmental entity or private person regarding a
governmental decision referenced in Title 2, California
Code of Regulations, section 18701(a)(2XA); or
"(b) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decisionmaker either directly or without significant
intervening substantive review, by:
"(l) Conducting research or making any investigation [that]
requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official
and the pllrpose of which is to influence a governmental
decision referenced in Title 2, California Code of
Regulations, section 187 0 1 (a)(2XA); or
"(2) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis. or
opinion, orally, or in writing, Ithatl requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of


File No. I-08-075
Pa-ee No.5
which is to influence a govemmental decision referenced in
Title 2, California Code of Regulations, section
I 870 1 (a)(2XA)." (Regulation 187 02.2.)
Making or parlicipating in making a governmental decision does not include actions
of public officials that are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual, or clerical. (Regulation
18702.4 (aXl).
Using or Attempting to Use Your Official Position to Influence a Decision
A public official is attempting to Llse his or her position to influence a decision
whenever:
"(a) With regard to a governmental decision which is
within or before an official's agency or an agency appointed
by or subject to the budgetary control of his or her agency,
the official is attempting to Lrse his or her official position
to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing
the decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or
otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to
influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or
contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity,
client, or customer.
"(b) With regard to a governmental decision which is
within or before an agency not covered by subsection (a),
the official is attempting to use his or her official position
to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing
the decision, the official acts or purports to act on behalf of,
or as the representative of, his or her agency to any
member, officer, employee or consultant of an agency.
Such actions include, but are not limited to the use of
official stationery." (Regulation 1 8702.3.)
Your facts indicate that you will not be acting as a decisionmaker in this process,
nor will you be taking any actions that would be classified as using or attempting to use
your position to influence a decision. This leaves the only question of whether or not your
actions would be considered as participating in a governmental decision under Regulation
18702.2 (b). This determination is based on whether or not there will be significant
intervening substantive review of your recommendations.
The most extensive discussion of "significant intervening substantive review" can
be for-rnd in the Kuplun Advice Letter, No. A-82-108 (copy enclosed). In that letter, we
explained that the Commission interpreted this exception namowly, and that an official


File No. I-08-075
Page No.6
"participates in a decision, even if it is 'reviewed' by several of his superiors, if those
superiors rely on the data or analysis prepared by the [official] without checking it
independently, if they rely on the professional judgment of the [official], or if the [official]
in sonte otlter w'u1t ttctuully mcty influerrce tlrc tlecisiott." (emphasis added.) we further
stated:
"Actions by public officials [thatl are nor inrended or likely
to influence a governmental decision (because the issues of
type of discretion or judgment involved in the actions is
unrelated to the outcome of the particular governmental
decision), do not constitute participating in that decision."
This determination in made on a case-by-case basis according to the facts pertaining
to the particular matter. If, after examining the factors outlined in Kaplan, you need ftrrther
assistance in determining whether or not you are participating in a governmental decision,
please write for further advice and provide the relevant details pertaining to your actions.
Srnp 3: Do You H.l,vn A PoTENTTALLv DrsquarrryrNc EcoNoMrc Inrrnnsr?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of section
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:
o
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Reg. 18703.1(a)); or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87103(d); Reg. 18703. l(b));
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Reg. 18703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c);
Reg. 18703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e): Reg. 18703.4);
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule
(Section 87103: Reg. 18703.5).
You have indicated that you have "a previous employee/employer relationship with
American Medical Response," and you were employed there throurgh December 2007. As
a
a
a
a


File No. I-08-075
Page No.7
such, yott would ordinarily have an economic interest in your former employer as a soLrrce
of income to you if you received more than $500 in the twelve months preceding any
governmental decision.
However, Regulation 18703.2 (b) provides an exception for former employers when
all income was accrLled before the official began his or her public service:
"(b) Former employers. Source of income, as used in
Government Code [S]ection 87103(c) and this section, shall
not include a former employer if: All income from the
employer was received by or accrued to the public official
prior to the time he or she became a public official; the
income was received in the normal course of the previous
employment; and there was no expectation by the public
official at the time he or she assumed office of renewed
employment with the former employer."
Because you have not provided any facts that indicate any other potential
economic interests, our analysis is limited to the economic interests identified above. If
you had no expectation of returning to your former employer at the time you took you
current position and all income from the former employer was received by or accrued
to you before you began you current employment, the analysis ends here, as, under the
facts presented, you to do have an economic interest that may be subject to a conflict of
interest. we are providing further analysis of steps four through eight for your
consideration only if you determine that you do not meet the exception for former
employers.
Srnp 4: Is Tur EcoNonuc Inmnpsr DtRrcrly lNvolvro rN THE GovBnNtTENTAL
DpcrsroN?
"[n order to determine if a govemmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the official's
economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the governmental decision."
(Regulation 1870a(a).) For economic interests in a source of income, Regulation I8704-l
determines the applicable material standard. (Regularion 1870a(aX1).)
Regulation 187 04.1 (a) states:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of
income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or by an agent:


File No. t-08-075
Page No. 8
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's
agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person."
Regulation 18704.1(b) then provides that if a source of income is directly involved,
the materiality standards set forth in 18705.1(b) apply. If the source of income is not
directly involved, the materiality standards set forth in 18705.1(c) apply. (Regulation
t8704.1(b).)
Under the facts presented, your former employer, American Medical Response, is
not a named party or the subject of the proceeding and is, therefore, not directly involved.
If American Medical Response becomes a bidder in the process, at that point it would be
directly involved. You have indicated that you will not be involved in any decisions
regarding the actually selection process. Accordingly, we proceed with our analysis
regarding materiality under the "not directly involved" standard.
Srrp 5: M.q,tpnInlrrY STANDARD
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation 1 8700(a).)
Source of Income:
For a source of income that is a business entity and not directly involved in a
governmental decision, Regulation 18705.1(c) provides for materiality standards that vary
according to the financial size of the business. By way of example, for relatively small
businesses (those with net income of less than $500,000 for the most recent fiscal year) the
materiality standards set forth in Regulation 18705.1(cX4) apply:
"(4) If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions
(cX1)-(3), the financial effect of a governmental decision
on the business entity is material if it is reasonably
foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a
fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more; or,


File No. I-08-075
Page No.9
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business
entity incnming or avoiding additional expenses or reducing
or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or
liabilities of $20,000 or more."
You have not indicated the financial size of your former employer so we are unable
to determine which materiality standard would be applicable if you determine that this
economic interest in not directly involved in the governmental decision.
For a source of income that is directly involved in the governmental decision any
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on that person is deemed material. (Regulation
18705.3(a).)
Srnp 6: RnasoNanly FoRESEEABLE
An effect Llpon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there is
a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) Whether the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is
made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need not be certain
to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re
Thorner (1975) t FPPC Ops. 198.)
srnps T lNn 8: Purlrc GpNrnar,r,y AND Lrc,lI,r,y Rnqunro p,rnrrcparroN
You have not presented any facts indicating that either the "public generally" or the
"legally required participation" exception would be applicable here. Accoidingly, we have
not provided an analysis involving these steps.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
By: William
it
wJLjgl
Enclosures
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

l. No. The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply to all public officials,
whether or not they are covered in the agency's contlict-of-interest code.
2. No. You will not have a conflict of interest if you do not make, participate in
making, or Llse your official position to influence a govemmental decision that will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of your economic interests, as
explained below. Furthermore, as your former employment was terminated before you

accepted you current position and all income from the former employer was received by or

sections.facts:
You are an employee of the County of Sonoma serving in the capacity of
"Advanced Life Support Coordinator" for the Coastal Valleys Emergency Medical Services
Agency, a regional regulatory agency that oversees prehospital medical services including
911 medical systems for Sonoma, Napa, and Mendocino Counties.
Your routine responsibilities include: policy and treatment protocol development;
continuous quality improvement program management; investigation and follow-up with
clinical issues; development and support of on-going training programs; management of the
agency's continuing education provider program; management of the agency's public
safety AED program; oversight of the certification process for EMT-1 providers and the
accreditation process for paramedic providers; ALS ambulance inspections; attendance at
regional and county meetings including the regional Medical Advisory Committee and the
county-specific Emergency Medical Services Advisory Committees for Napa, Sonoma, and
Mendocino Counties.
Pursuant to Section 87302, your employer has enumerated the positions within the
colrnty that involve the making or participation in the making of decisions that may have a
material effect on any financial interest. Several years ago, your employer reviewed your
position and determined that it shor.rld not be included as a designated position.
Consequently, you do not file a Fair Political Practices Form 700 (Statement of Economic
Interests). You have been advised that "this fact ordinarily would preclude [you] from
having a Political Reform Act conflict of interest." However, yoll are reqllesting our advice
regarding this issue.
Sonoma County is in the process of preparing a Request for Proposal (RFP) for
exclttsive rights to operate Advanced Life Support service within the Santa Rosa area
pursLrant to California Health and Safety Code Section 1797.224. You are being
considered to take part in the group that will make recommendations regarding the RFP
and work on the project. Your work will be supervised by the EMS Agency
Administrator, the EMS Agency Medical Director, the Sonoma County Public Health
Officer, and the Sonoma County Director of Health Services. The ultimate contract
will be reviewed and approved by the Sonoma County Board of Supervisors. You will
be serving in an advisory capacity to the Department of Health Services as an EMS
agency representative and sharing the agency's viewpoint with the project team. You
will not be serving on the selection committee or making recommendations as to which
bidder will be awarded the contract.
You have a previons employment relationship with American Medical Response,
one of the likely bidders for the potential contract. You were employed by AMR through
December 2007. You do not have any other financial relationship with AMR.

when the official, acting within the ar.rthority of his or her
office or position:

action;
"(4) Enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his
or her agency;
"(5) Determines not to act, within the meaning of
subdivisions (a)(1), (a)(2), (a)(3), or (a)(4), above, unless
such determination is made because of his or her financial
interest." (Regulation 18702.1.)
Participating in a Governmental Decision:
"A public official 'participates in making a governmental
decision,' ..., when, acting within the authority of his or

opinion, orally, or in writing, Ithatl requires the exercise of

which is to influence a govemmental decision referenced in
Title 2, California Code of Regulations, section
I 870 1 (a)(2XA)." (Regulation 187 02.2.)

of public officials that are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual, or clerical. (Regulation

Using or Attempting to Use Your Official Position to Influence a Decision
A public official is attempting to Llse his or her position to influence a decision
whenever:
"(a) With regard to a governmental decision which is
within or before an official's agency or an agency appointed
by or subject to the budgetary control of his or her agency,
the official is attempting to Lrse his or her official position
to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing
the decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or
otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to
influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or
contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity,
client, or customer.
"(b) With regard to a governmental decision which is
within or before an agency not covered by subsection (a),
the official is attempting to use his or her official position
to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing
the decision, the official acts or purports to act on behalf of,
or as the representative of, his or her agency to any
member, officer, employee or consultant of an agency.
Such actions include, but are not limited to the use of
official stationery." (Regulation 1 8702.3.)
Your facts indicate that you will not be acting as a decisionmaker in this process,
nor will you be taking any actions that would be classified as using or attempting to use
your position to influence a decision. This leaves the only question of whether or not your
actions would be considered as participating in a governmental decision under Regulation
18702.2 (b). This determination is based on whether or not there will be significant

in sonte otlter w'u1t ttctuully mcty influerrce tlrc tlecisiott." (emphasis added.) we further
stated:
"Actions by public officials [thatl are nor inrended or likely
to influence a governmental decision (because the issues of
type of discretion or judgment involved in the actions is
unrelated to the outcome of the particular governmental
decision), do not constitute participating in that decision."
This determination in made on a case-by-case basis according to the facts pertaining
to the particular matter. If, after examining the factors outlined in Kaplan, you need ftrrther
assistance in determining whether or not you are participating in a governmental decision,
please write for further advice and provide the relevant details pertaining to your actions.

A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of section
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:
o
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Reg. 18703.1(a)); or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87103(d); Reg. 18703. l(b));
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Reg. 18703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c);
Reg. 18703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e): Reg. 18703.4);
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule
(Section 87103: Reg. 18703.5).
You have indicated that you have "a previous employee/employer relationship with
American Medical Response," and you were employed there throurgh December 2007. As
a
a
a
a
such, yott would ordinarily have an economic interest in your former employer as a soLrrce
of income to you if you received more than $500 in the twelve months preceding any
governmental decision.
However, Regulation 18703.2 (b) provides an exception for former employers when
all income was accrLled before the official began his or her public service:
"(b) Former employers. Source of income, as used in
Government Code [S]ection 87103(c) and this section, shall
not include a former employer if: All income from the
employer was received by or accrued to the public official
prior to the time he or she became a public official; the
income was received in the normal course of the previous
employment; and there was no expectation by the public
official at the time he or she assumed office of renewed
employment with the former employer."
Because you have not provided any facts that indicate any other potential
economic interests, our analysis is limited to the economic interests identified above. If
you had no expectation of returning to your former employer at the time you took you
current position and all income from the former employer was received by or accrued
to you before you began you current employment, the analysis ends here, as, under the
facts presented, you to do have an economic interest that may be subject to a conflict of
interest. we are providing further analysis of steps four through eight for your
consideration only if you determine that you do not meet the exception for former
employers.
Srnp 4: Is Tur EcoNonuc Inmnpsr DtRrcrly lNvolvro rN THE GovBnNtTENTAL
DpcrsroN?
"[n order to determine if a govemmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the official's
economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the governmental decision."
(Regulation 1870a(a).) For economic interests in a source of income, Regulation I8704-l
determines the applicable material standard. (Regularion 1870a(aX1).)
Regulation 187 04.1 (a) states:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of
income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or by an agent:


File No. t-08-075
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's
agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person."
Regulation 18704.1(b) then provides that if a source of income is directly involved,
the materiality standards set forth in 18705.1(b) apply. If the source of income is not
directly involved, the materiality standards set forth in 18705.1(c) apply. (Regulation
t8704.1(b).)
Under the facts presented, your former employer, American Medical Response, is
not a named party or the subject of the proceeding and is, therefore, not directly involved.
If American Medical Response becomes a bidder in the process, at that point it would be
directly involved. You have indicated that you will not be involved in any decisions
regarding the actually selection process. Accordingly, we proceed with our analysis
regarding materiality under the "not directly involved" standard.
Srrp 5: M.q,tpnInlrrY STANDARD
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation 1 8700(a).)
Source of Income:
For a source of income that is a business entity and not directly involved in a
governmental decision, Regulation 18705.1(c) provides for materiality standards that vary
according to the financial size of the business. By way of example, for relatively small
businesses (those with net income of less than $500,000 for the most recent fiscal year) the
materiality standards set forth in Regulation 18705.1(cX4) apply:
"(4) If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions
(cX1)-(3), the financial effect of a governmental decision
on the business entity is material if it is reasonably
foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a
fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business
entity incnming or avoiding additional expenses or reducing
or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or
liabilities of $20,000 or more."
You have not indicated the financial size of your former employer so we are unable
to determine which materiality standard would be applicable if you determine that this
economic interest in not directly involved in the governmental decision.
For a source of income that is directly involved in the governmental decision any
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on that person is deemed material. (Regulation
18705.3(a).)
Srnp 6: RnasoNanly FoRESEEABLE
An effect Llpon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there is
a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) Whether the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is
made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need not be certain
to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re
Thorner (1975) t FPPC Ops. 198.)
srnps T lNn 8: Purlrc GpNrnar,r,y AND Lrc,lI,r,y Rnqunro p,rnrrcparroN
You have not presented any facts indicating that either the "public generally" or the
"legally required participation" exception would be applicable here. Accoidingly, we have
not provided an analysis involving these steps.
